text: This will be a fascinating experiment. RF oscillators will achieve GHz frequencies, but this brings the unintended thought that perhaps the circuit experience a loss due to RF emissions.  A Faraday cage might be in order for testing.

author: @DrSaminstine
text: This is going in a great direction I have been playing with this idea too for a while now I know it's too hard to reach these speeds mechanically but the collapse of magnetic fields and induction from a rare earth magnet passing the conductor may play a role in adding extra electron flow so adding a mechanical oscillation might give a favorable result I have been trying to work out how to do this at such high frequencies and what I have come up with is harmonics. Just like a musical scale the notes are vibrating at a certain attenuation and each note has a harmony at lower and higher frequency so if we ran a frequency in the gig or meg range there is most certainly a harmonic in the lower hertz range that a rotor with those magnets could sync up with if spun up Manually and disengaged at the right harmonic frequency might act like the mass on the swing while inducing extra energy to the system thanks again for sharing and inspiring!!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: DrSaminstine that's a fascinating plan mate - let me know how you get on

author: @cheerdiver
text:
When in an automation lab, we were shown how it's possible to set up an AC "charge" between two capacitors.  This could be seen on the O-scope, but was of very low current.  It could remain in place for long periods of time.  A problem arises in supercaps, as they only take a certain polarity (going by how commercial caps have a polarity designation).  Though I fail to see how parasitically removing "energy" wouldn't just act as a "shock absorber", removing the oscillation.
As Tesla attested, there is energy all around us, it just a matter of harvesting it.  That Automation/Robotics instructor, did experiments on harvesting lightning at NCSU.  Those experiments came to an end when a student walked into the lightning recreation room while the capacitor bank still had a partial charge, RIP.
